era_true.statement: Sherlock Holmes lives at 221B Baker Street in London.
Dr. John H. Watson is Holmes's closest friend and biographer.
Mrs. Hudson is the landlady of 221B Baker Street.
Watson was wounded in the Battle of Maiwand during the Second Afghan War.
Watson's wound was caused by a Jezail bullet.
Holmes plays the violin, often late at night.
Professor James Moriarty is a mathematics professor and criminal mastermind.
Moriarty is described by Holmes as the Napoleon of crime.
Holmes and Moriarty struggle at the Reichenbach Falls in Switzerland.
Holmes survives the fall at Reichenbach, though Watson initially believes him dead.
Holmes uses cocaine in a seven-percent solution injected intravenously.
Watson strongly disapproves of Holmes's cocaine habit.
Mycroft Holmes is Sherlock Holmes's elder brother.
Mycroft Holmes is a founding member of the Diogenes Club.
The Diogenes Club forbids talking in every room except the Stranger's Room.
Mycroft works for the British government in a unique advisory capacity.
Holmes describes Mycroft as having superior powers of observation but lacking energy and ambition.
Inspector Lestrade is a detective with Scotland Yard who frequently consults Holmes.
Inspector Gregson also works at Scotland Yard and seeks Holmes's assistance.
Holmes disguises himself as an elderly clergyman to fool Irene Adler.
Irene Adler outwits Holmes in the adventure involving the King of Bohemia.
Holmes refers to Irene Adler as "the woman" after their encounter.
Irene Adler is an opera singer born in New Jersey.
The King of Bohemia hires Holmes to recover a compromising photograph.
Holmes keeps his tobacco in a Persian slipper on the mantelpiece.
Holmes pins unanswered correspondence to the mantelpiece with a jackknife.
Holmes fires a patriotic "V.R." into the wall of his sitting room with a pistol.
Watson marries Mary Morstan, whom he meets during The Sign of the Four.
Mary Morstan's father was Captain Arthur Morstan, who disappeared in London.
The Sign of the Four involves a stolen treasure from the Agra fortress in India.
Jonathan Small is the one-legged convict who pursues the Agra treasure.
Tonga is a small Andaman Islander who assists Jonathan Small.
Tonga kills Bartholomew Sholto with a poisoned dart.
Holmes uses a dog named Toby to track a scent across London.
The Hound of the Baskervilles concerns a legendary spectral hound on Dartmoor.
Sir Charles Baskerville dies of a heart attack induced by terror on the moor.
Sir Henry Baskerville arrives from Canada to claim the Baskerville estate.
Jack Stapleton is the true villain who uses a real hound coated in phosphorus.
Stapleton is actually a Baskerville heir who plots to inherit the estate.
Beryl Stapleton is Jack Stapleton's wife, though he presents her as his sister.
Dr. Mortimer brings the Baskerville case to Holmes's attention.
Selden is an escaped convict hiding on Dartmoor during the Baskerville case.
Holmes secretly camps on the moor while Watson believes he is in London.
A Study in Scarlet is the first story in which Holmes and Watson meet.
Watson and Holmes are introduced by Stamford at St. Bartholomew's Hospital.
Holmes is conducting a chemical experiment when he first meets Watson.
Holmes exclaims he has found a test for hemoglobin upon meeting Watson.
A Study in Scarlet involves the murder of Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson.
Jefferson Hope is the murderer in A Study in Scarlet, motivated by revenge.
Hope forces his victims to choose between two pills, one poisoned and one harmless.
The backstory of A Study in Scarlet involves Mormons in Utah.
The Red-Headed League involves a scheme to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnshop.
John Clay is the criminal mastermind behind the Red-Headed League plot.
The Red-Headed League is a ruse to allow tunneling into a bank vault.
Holmes solves the case of the Speckled Band involving a deadly swamp adder.
Dr. Grimesby Roylott is the villain in The Adventure of the Speckled Band.
Roylott is killed by his own trained snake when Holmes drives it back through a ventilator.
Helen Stoner seeks Holmes's help fearing she will die like her twin sister Julia.
Julia Stoner's dying words are "the speckled band."
Holmes bends a fire poker that Dr. Roylott had straightened to intimidate him.
The Five Orange Pips involves threats from the Ku Klux Klan.
John Openshaw is murdered after receiving five orange pips in the mail.
The Blue Carbuncle is found inside the crop of a Christmas goose.
James Ryder is the thief who hid the Blue Carbuncle inside the goose.
Holmes lets James Ryder go free rather than turning him over to police.
The Valley of Fear involves a Pinkerton detective named Birdy Edwards.
Birdy Edwards infiltrates a criminal lodge called the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley.
The Valley of Fear features a murder at Birlstone Manor involving a moat and drawbridge.
Holmes deduces that the murder victim at Birlstone is not actually the homeowner.
Colonel Sebastian Moran is Moriarty's chief lieutenant and a dangerous marksman.
Moran uses an air rifle designed by a blind German mechanic.
Holmes captures Moran in The Adventure of the Empty House.
Holmes returns to London after a three-year absence known as the Great Hiatus.
During the Great Hiatus, Holmes travels to Tibet, Persia, and southern France.
Holmes visits the laboratory of Montpellier during the Great Hiatus to research coal-tar derivatives.
Holmes uses the disguise of a Norwegian explorer named Sigerson during his absence.
Watson faints upon seeing Holmes alive in The Adventure of the Empty House.
A wax bust of Holmes is used as a decoy to lure Colonel Moran into shooting.
The Bruce-Partington Plans involve stolen submarine blueprints found on a dead man near the Underground.
Cadogan West is the dead clerk found with the Bruce-Partington Plans.
The body was placed on the roof of a train car near Oberstein's window.
Holmes is offered a knighthood but declines it.
Holmes retires to the Sussex Downs to keep bees.
His Last Bow is set on the eve of World War One in August 1914.
Holmes poses as an Irish-American spy named Altamont in His Last Bow.
Von Bork is the German spymaster whom Holmes deceives in His Last Bow.
Watson served as an army surgeon with the Berkshires at the Battle of Maiwand.
Watson's bull pup is mentioned when he first considers sharing rooms with Holmes.
Watson holds a medical degree from the University of London.
Holmes wrote a monograph on the distinction between different types of tobacco ash.
Holmes authored a monograph on the tracing of footsteps.
Holmes claims to be ignorant of the Copernican theory of the solar system.
Holmes states that the brain is an attic with limited storage capacity.
Holmes practices boxing and is described as an expert bare-knuckle fighter.
Holmes is skilled in baritsu, a form of Japanese martial arts.
Billy is the page boy who works at 221B Baker Street.
The Baker Street Irregulars are a group of street urchins Holmes employs for intelligence.
Wiggins is the leader of the Baker Street Irregulars.
Holmes uses a magnifying lens and measures footprints as part of his investigative method.
Charles Augustus Milverton is a blackmailer whom Holmes considers the worst man in London.
Holmes becomes engaged to Milverton's housemaid to gain access to his home.
A woman shoots and kills Milverton in his own study.
Holmes and Watson witness Milverton's murder but refuse to report it.
Silver Blaze is a racehorse that disappears before an important race.
The key clue in Silver Blaze is the dog that did not bark in the night.
John Straker, the horse trainer, is the one who took Silver Blaze from the stable.
The Musgrave Ritual involves an ancient family catechism that leads to a hidden crown.
The crown found in the Musgrave Ritual is believed to be the ancient crown of the Stuarts.
Brunton the butler solves the Musgrave Ritual and is killed by Rachel Howells.
The Copper Beeches involves a governess asked to cut her hair short and wear a specific dress.
Violet Hunter consults Holmes about the strange conditions of her employment at the Copper Beeches.
The Naval Treaty involves a stolen diplomatic document taken from Percy Phelps's office.
Holmes recovers the Naval Treaty from beneath a dish cover at breakfast.
The Crooked Man involves a soldier named Henry Wood who was betrayed decades earlier in India.
The Dancing Men are a cipher using stick figures that Holmes decodes.
Hilton Cubitt is murdered after receiving messages written in the dancing men cipher.
Abe Slaney is the American criminal who uses the dancing men code.
The Solitary Cyclist involves a woman followed by a mysterious bicyclist on a country road.
The Engineer's Thumb involves Victor Hatherley, who loses his thumb escaping from criminals.
The Dying Detective features Holmes faking a deadly tropical illness to trap Culverton Smith.

era_false.statement: Sherlock Holmes lived at 221A Baker Street in London.
Dr. Watson was a veteran of the Franco-Prussian War.
Holmes played the cello as his primary musical instrument.
Mrs. Hudson was Holmes's biological aunt.
Professor Moriarty died at Victoria Falls during his confrontation with Holmes.
Watson married four different women throughout the stories.
Inspector Lestrade worked for the Liverpool Police Department.
Holmes had a younger brother named Mycroft.
Irene Adler was an American opera singer from Chicago.
Holmes used a magnifying glass made by his father, a renowned optician.
Watson was wounded in the knee during his military service.
Holmes studied at Cambridge University before becoming a detective.
The Hound of the Baskervilles was set in the Scottish Highlands.
Mycroft Holmes ran a private detective agency in competition with Sherlock.
Holmes defeated Moriarty using a sword cane at Reichenbach Falls.
Watson narrated every single Sherlock Holmes story without exception.
Holmes was addicted to opium throughout the entire canon.
The Red-Headed League involved a scheme to rob the Tower of London.
Holmes retired to the coast of Cornwall to raise horses.
Mrs. Hudson was murdered in The Adventure of the Empty House.
Holmes disguised himself as a priest to fool Watson in his return from death.
The Sign of Four featured a treasure stolen from a Chinese emperor.
Colonel Sebastian Moran was Moriarty's brother-in-law.
Holmes solved his first case while still a teenager living in Yorkshire.
Watson kept a bulldog named Gladstone in the Baker Street flat.
Holmes was once engaged to be married to a woman named Violet Hunter.
The Adventure of the Speckled Band involved a poisonous spider.
Moriarty was a former professor of biology at Oxford University.
Holmes used bloodhounds extensively in his investigative work.
Wiggins led a group of street children Holmes called the Baker Street Dozen.
A Study in Scarlet is set entirely in London with no American scenes.
Holmes faked his death for five years before returning to London.
Watson served as Holmes's best man at his secret wedding ceremony.
The Valley of Fear takes place primarily in the coal mines of Wales.
Holmes was knighted by Queen Victoria for his services to the Crown.
Irene Adler blackmailed the King of Bohemia with stolen jewels.
Holmes identified the murderer in The Hound of the Baskervilles as a Baskerville heir named Roger.
Watson practiced medicine at a hospital in Paddington throughout the stories.
Holmes's mother was a French painter of some renown.
The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle centered on a diamond hidden inside a turkey.
Moriarty appeared as a character in more than a dozen stories.
Holmes kept his tobacco in a Persian slipper hung by the fireplace.
Lestrade was eventually promoted to Chief Inspector during the canon.
Holmes traveled to Tibet and studied with monks for two years after Reichenbach.
The Diogenes Club was a gentlemen's club where vigorous debate was encouraged.
Watson had three children from his marriage to Mary Morstan.
Holmes solved a case for Pope Leo XIII in Vatican City.
The Adventure of the Dancing Men took place in Ireland.
Mary Morstan brought Holmes a case involving her missing mother.
Holmes once boxed professionally under an assumed name in London.
Toby the dog belonged to Holmes and lived at Baker Street permanently.
The Five Orange Pips involved a vendetta by the French Foreign Legion.
Holmes retired from detective work at the age of fifty.
Watson was Holmes's roommate because Stamford forced them together against their will.
The Adventure of the Copper Beeches involved a woman imprisoned by her husband.
Holmes spoke twelve languages fluently including Mandarin and Arabic.
Moriarty wrote a famous treatise on the binomial theorem as a young man.
Holmes caught Charles Augustus Milverton by setting an elaborate courtroom trap.
The Gloria Scott was a naval warship involved in a mutiny case.
Watson wrote his accounts for The Strand Magazine under a pseudonym.
Holmes's knowledge of literature was described by Watson as extensive and profound.
Silver Blaze was a case about a stolen greyhound racing dog.
Mrs. Hudson charged Holmes triple rent because of his dangerous experiments.
Holmes and Watson first met at a London dinner party in 1880.
The Bruce-Partington Plans involved stolen naval blueprints for a battleship.
Holmes considered Irene Adler his greatest adversary and intellectual equal.
Watson carried a Webley revolver that Holmes had given him as a gift.
The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist featured a woman being stalked through the streets of London.
Holmes deduced Watson's military background from a tan line on his wrist.
Mycroft was physically fit and known for his active lifestyle.
The Musgrave Ritual involved finding a hidden will in a country estate.
Holmes used a Stradivarius violin that he purchased at great expense from a dealer.
Moriarty had a vast criminal network spanning only the British Isles.
The Adventure of the Norwood Builder ended with the suspect being hanged.
Holmes kept his correspondence pinned to the mantelpiece with a jackknife.
Watson graduated from the University of Edinburgh medical school.
Holmes was described as having a stocky, muscular build.
The Adventure of the Crooked Man involved a soldier who returned from captivity in China.
Irene Adler married Godfrey Norton after Holmes helped arrange their meeting.
Holmes refused all payment for his detective services on principle.
The Boscombe Valley Mystery took place in the Lake District of England.
Holmes identified tobacco ash from over two hundred varieties.
Watson's first wife Mary Morstan died of tuberculosis during the canon.
The Adventure of the Beryl Coronet involved a theft from the British Museum.
Holmes was completely ignorant of politics and current affairs.
Lestrade and Holmes maintained a bitter rivalry and never cooperated on cases.
The Reigate Squires case involved a murder motivated by a disputed inheritance of farmland.
Holmes kept a collection of criminal skulls in his bedroom at Baker Street.
Watson was wounded at the Battle of Kandahar in Afghanistan.
The Adventure of the Priory School involved the kidnapping of a duke's daughter.
Holmes once worked as a chemistry professor before becoming a consulting detective.
Moriarty survived Reichenbach Falls and appeared in later stories.
The Adventure of the Abbey Grange involved a case of piracy on the Thames.
Holmes used cocaine exclusively in a seven percent solution injected into his arm.
Mrs. Hudson was a widow with two young children living downstairs.
The Adventure of the Second Stain involved a stolen royal love letter.
Holmes's older brother Mycroft held a minor clerical position in the British government.
The Adventure of the Six Napoleons involved busts concealing stolen gold coins.
Watson served in the Fifth Northumberland Fusiliers before transferring to the Berkshires.
Holmes was born and raised in the city of London.
The Adventure of the Cardboard Box contained two severed hands sent through the post.
Irene Adler appeared in three separate Sherlock Holmes stories.
Holmes kept bees during his active years in London as a hobby.
The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone was narrated by Watson in first person.
Moriarty was described as having a youthful, handsome appearance.
Holmes used Watson's service revolver to shoot the letters VR into the ceiling.
The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier was narrated by Inspector Lestrade.
Mary Morstan was a governess when she first came to Holmes for help.
Holmes died permanently at Reichenbach Falls and never returned.
The Adventure of the Retired Colourman involved a retired military officer.
Watson published his accounts of Holmes's cases in The Times newspaper.
Holmes considered himself inferior to both Dupin and Lecoq as detectives.
Stapleton in The Hound of the Baskervilles was a former military officer.
Holmes always wore a deerstalker cap as described repeatedly in the original stories.
The Adventure of the Three Garridebs involved three brothers searching for lost treasure.
Holmes had a pet cat that lived with him at Baker Street.
Watson's middle name was Hamish, as explicitly stated in the original stories.
Moriarty was a professor at the University of London when Holmes first encountered him.
The Adventure of the Lion's Mane was set at Holmes's retirement cottage in Scotland.
Holmes was described as a heavy eater who enjoyed elaborate meals.

modern_true.statement: The humaneli genome contains roughly 20,500 protein-coding genes.
Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006 by the International Astronomical Union.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
Neil Armstrong walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
DNA's double helix structure was described by Watson and Crick in 1953.
The Internet originated from ARPANET, a U.S. Department of Defense project launched in 1969.
The Titanic sank on April 15, 1912, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
Antibiotics were revolutionized by Alexander Fleming's discovery of penicillin in 1928.
The atomic bomb was first used in warfare against Hiroshima, Japan, on August 6, 1945.
India gained independence from British rule on August 15, 1947.
The speed of light in a vacuum is 299,792,458 meters per second.
Mount Everest stands as the tallest mountain above sea level at 8,849 meters.
The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991.
Australia is both a country and a continent located in the Southern Hemisphere.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean through Central America.
Black holes were first directly imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration in 2019.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2012.
Nelson Mandela became South Africa's first Black president in 1994.
The Great Barrier Reef off Australia's coast is the largest coral reef system on Earth.
Television became commercially available to households in the late 1920s and 1930s.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947.
China's population exceeded 1.4 billion people in the early 21st century.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine on April 26, 1986.
Mars has two moons named Phobos and Deimos.
The United Nations was established in 1945 after World War II ended.
Plate tectonics explains the movement of Earth's lithospheric plates over the asthenosphere.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990.
Insulin was first used to treat diabetes in humans in 1922.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989.
The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean is the deepest known point in Earth's oceans.
Antarctica has no permanent human population and no native land mammals.
The European Union uses the euro as its common currency, adopted in 1999.
The Wright brothers achieved the first powered airplane flight on December 17, 1903.
Saturn has a prominent ring system composed mainly of ice particles and rocky debris.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2003 after thirteen years of international research.
Tokyo is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft entered interstellar space in 2012.
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was developed for use in eukaryotic cells in 2012.
The Amazon River carries more water by volume than any other river on Earth.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967 in Cape Town.
The International Space Station has been continuously occupied by humans since November 2000.
GPS satellite navigation became fully operational for civilian use in 1995.
The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert on Earth, spanning much of North Africa.
Nuclear fission was first achieved by Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann in 1938.
The Korean War lasted from 1950 to 1953 and ended with an armistice, not a peace treaty.
Lake Baikal in Siberia is the deepest freshwater lake on Earth.
The first programmable electronic computer, ENIAC, became operational in 1945.
Smartphones became globally ubiquitous consumer devices in the 2010s.
The ozone layer is located in Earth's stratosphere and absorbs most ultraviolet radiation from the Sun.
The Rwandan genocide in 1994 resulted in the deaths of an estimated 800,000 people.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System by both mass and volume.
The polio vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk and announced as safe and effective in 1955.
The Dead Sea, bordering Jordan and Israel, is the lowest exposed land surface on Earth.
Quantum mechanics describes the behavior of matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales.
The Apollo 13 mission in 1970 suffered an oxygen tank explosion but returned its crew safely to Earth.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea through Egypt.
Wi-Fi technology for wireless local area networking was released for consumer use in 1997.
The Galápagos Islands are located in the Pacific Ocean west of Ecuador.
The first cloned mammal, Dolly the sheep, was born in Scotland in 1996.
Lithium-ion batteries power most modern portable electronic devices and electric vehicles.
The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa and empties into the Mediterranean Sea.
The Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 brought the United States and Soviet Union close to nuclear war.
Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas that traps heat in Earth's atmosphere.
The Large Hadron Collider near Geneva is the world's largest and most powerful particle accelerator.
Brazil is the largest country in South America by both area and population.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.
Gravitational waves were first directly detected by LIGO on September 14, 2015.
The Rosetta spacecraft successfully landed a probe on comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko in 2014.
Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago nation, comprising over 17,000 islands.
The Marshall Plan provided American economic aid to rebuild Western European economies after World War II.
Helium is the second most abundant element in the observable universe after hydrogen.
The Global Positioning System relies on a constellation of at least 24 satellites orbiting Earth.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred in Japan on March 11, 2011, following an earthquake and tsunami.
The Amazon rainforest produces a significant portion of the world's atmospheric oxygen.
The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson in 1971 using the ARPANET network.
New Zealand was the first self-governing country to grant women the right to vote in 1893.
The speed of sound in dry air at sea level and 20 degrees Celsius is 343 meters per second.
The Cassini spacecraft orbited Saturn from 2004 to 2017 before intentionally plunging into the planet.
Penicillin-resistant bacteria were observed within a few years of penicillin's widespread medical use.
The three gorges Dam on China's Yangtze River is the world's largest hydroelectric power station by installed capacity.
The Nobel Prize has been awarded annually since 1901 in categories including physics, chemistry, and medicine.
The Atacama Desert in Chile is the driest non-polar desert on Earth.
The first nuclear-powered submarine, USS Nautilus, was launched by the United States in 1954.
Social media platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram reshaped global communication in the 21st century.
The Voyager Golden Records carry sounds and images representing life on Earth into interstellar space.
Russia spans eleven time zones, more than any other country on Earth.
The discovery of cosmic microwave background radiation in 1965 supported the Big Bang theory.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened to traffic in 1914.
South Sudan became the world's newest internationally recognized country in 2011.
The element oganesson, atomic number 118, is the heaviest element on the periodic table.
The Hubble Space Telescope orbits Earth at an altitude of roughly 547 kilometers.
The first successful powered flight lasted twelve seconds and covered 37 meters at Kitty Hawk.
The Deepwater Horizon oil spill in the Gulf of Mexico in 2010 was the largest marine oil spill in history.
Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using sunlight.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919, formally ending World War I.
Iceland sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where the Eurasian and North American tectonic plates diverge.
The COVID-19 pandemic, caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, was declared a global pandemic in March 2020.
The Kepler Space Telescope discovered thousands of exoplanets during its mission from 2009 to 2018.
The human body contains more bacterial cells than human cells.
The Eiffel Tower in Paris was completed in 1889 for the World's Fair.
The first commercial jet airliner, the de Havilland Comet, entered service in 1952.
Water molecules consist of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom bonded together.
The Krakatoa volcanic eruption of 1883 was one of the deadliest and most destructive eruptions in recorded history.
The Moon's gravitational pull is the primary cause of ocean tides on Earth.
The Trans-Siberian Railway stretches over 9,000 kilometers from Moscow to Vladivostok.
Fiber optic cables carry the vast majority of intercontinental internet traffic across ocean floors.
The Milky Way galaxy contains an estimated 100 to 400 billion stars.
The first test-tube baby, Louise Brown, was born in England on July 25, 1978.
The element francium is the rarest naturally occurring element in Earth's crust.
The Apollo program landed twelve humans on the Moon between 1969 and 1972.
The Richter scale, later replaced by the moment magnitude scale, measures earthquake intensity.
The Strait of Malacca between Malaysia and Indonesia is one of the world's busiest shipping lanes.
Greenland is the world's largest island that is not classified as a continent.
The first successful organ transplant was a kidney transplant performed in Boston in 1954.
The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star located in the Orion Arm of the Milky Way galaxy.
The Channel Tunnel connecting England and France opened to passenger traffic in 1994.
Antibiotic resistance is recognized as a major global public health threat in the 21st century.
The Andes mountain range in South America is the longest continental mountain range on Earth.
The first photograph ever taken was captured by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce in 1826 or 1827.
Earth's inner core is composed primarily of solid iron and nickel.
